missing_perspectives.who: B40 households across all ethnic communities
missing_perspectives.what: The headline drop in household damage obscures the fact that low-income families bear flood losses disproportionately relative to their assets. DOSM survey found 22.5% of victims made no prior preparations, only 5.5% had insurance. PIAM data shows >50% of Malaysian households uninsured against flood damage.
missing_perspectives.why: The analysis focuses on aggregate numbers and the public-private mix shift but never asks who within 'households' absorbed the RM183.8M in damage. Class cuts across race here.

missing_perspectives.who: East Malaysian indigenous communities (Kenyah, Penan, Kadazan-Dusun, Iban)
missing_perspectives.what: DOSM's data shows Sarawak recorded the highest living quarters losses (RM32.6M) and Sabah second (RM27.7M) in 2025. Indigenous communities in Sarawak's interior are suing logging firms for flood-exacerbating deforestation.
missing_perspectives.why: East Malaysia's damage pattern suggests a different causal story involving logging, land use change, and infrastructure neglect that the KL-centric framing misses.

missing_perspectives.who: Women, especially mothers and caregivers in flood-affected households
missing_perspectives.what: NADMA evacuation data shows women and children form >60% of evacuees. The two flood deaths in Kelantan in November 2025 were both women.
missing_perspectives.why: A damage-bill analysis that counts furniture but not care work, lost informal-sector income, or psychological trauma is gender-blind.

missing_perspectives.who: Orang Asli communities in Peninsular Malaysia
missing_perspectives.what: November 2024 saw ~400 Orang Asli in Pos Lebir, Gua Musang evacuated; Cameron Highlands flash floods displaced more. Often informal settlements undercounted in damage assessments.
missing_perspectives.why: RM636.9M may understate Orang Asli losses systematically.

missing_perspectives.what: 2025 Cambridge working paper documents recurrent flooding disproportionately impacts refugees in flood-prone affordable areas; excluded from formal relief.
missing_perspectives.why: DOSM survey unclear whether refugee/stateless households are captured.

missing_perspectives.what: Agriculture losses fell to RM52.6M (2025) from RM185.2M (2024). RM255M rubber smallholder flood relief + RM127.5M Monsoon Season Aid to 318,642 smallholders in 2024.
missing_perspectives.why: Good weather 2025 gave smallholders a reprieve, not resilience.

alternative_framings.current: The 31.8% drop in total damage is a story about 'whose balance sheet the damage showed up on,' implying a policy-relevant private-to-public burden shift.
alternative_framings.alternative: The 2025 drop may be almost entirely meteorological variance. Five-year DOSM data: 2021 RM6.1B, 2022 RM622M, 2023 RM755M, 2024 RM933M, 2025 RM637M. The 2021 figure is 10x the 2025 figure (the December 2021 catastrophic floods). No mitigation project completed 2024-2025 could plausibly explain a RM296M drop.
alternative_framings.evidence: DOSM multi-year data shows large year-to-year swings driven by whether a major event occurs in that calendar year.

alternative_framings.current: Rising public-asset damage share (33%→60%) is a shift in 'whose balance sheet' bears the cost.
alternative_framings.alternative: The rising public-asset share may reflect deteriorating under-maintained infrastructure that is increasingly vulnerable to even moderate flooding, not a genuine burden shift. PAC found PRAB Phase 1 cost RM129M but achieved only 5.6% forecasting accuracy. AG Report 2/2024: 145 of 712 water infrastructure projects delayed 86-1,076 days at RM6.15B cost.
alternative_framings.evidence: PAC March 2024 report on PRAB Phase 1 accuracy. Fadillah Yusof acknowledged 26 east coast flood mitigation projects (RM8B) delayed by land acquisition and utility relocation.

alternative_framings.current: 'Business interruption, productivity loss, deaths and prolonged displacement excluded' as a methodology footnote on Governance card.
alternative_framings.alternative: The exclusion is not a footnote; it means DOSM's RM636.9M may capture less than half of true economic cost. World Bank-BNM 4.1% GDP scenario implies a 1-in-20-year flood costs ~RM80B. International direct:total ratio typically 1:1.5 to 1:3 — true 2025 cost could be RM1.0-1.9B.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Disaster Pooling Fund (PFB) via Presidential Regulation 75/2021; includes state asset insurance program (ABMN) for government infrastructure. US$14M Global Shield grant.
international_parallels.lesson: Malaysia has no sovereign insurance mechanism for public infrastructure. Every ringgit of public-asset damage funded ex-post.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Parametric disaster insurance + catastrophe bonds. Layered DRFI strategy.
international_parallels.lesson: Malaysia is almost entirely ex-post budgetary.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Members: Cambodia, Indonesia, Japan, Laos, Myanmar, Philippines, Singapore, Vietnam. Malaysia is NOT a member. SEADRIF made US$2M payout to Laos in 6 business days Sept 2025. SAFE initiative launched 2026 for public infrastructure insurance.
international_parallels.lesson: Malaysia's non-membership is significant given public infrastructure now carries 60% of flood damage.

international_parallels.rule_or_statistic: Post-2011 floods (US$46.5B damage): National Catastrophe Insurance Fund 2012, government reinsurance backing, mandatory flood coverage.
international_parallels.lesson: Malaysia's 2021-2022 RM6.1B floods were a catalytic moment but unlike Thailand no national flood insurance scheme followed.

counter_arguments.counter: Five-year DOSM data shows annual damage dominated by whether a major event occurs. Attributing any portion of 2025 drop to policy success would be overclaim without meteorological analysis.
counter_arguments.source: my own reasoning based on DOSM multi-year data

counter_arguments.counter: The framing could imply households are better off when the more plausible interpretation is public infrastructure degrading faster. 2024 AG: 145 delayed water projects. PAC: RM129M flood warning system at 5.6% accuracy.
Card understates how narrow the number is. 'Narrowest number' framing without quantifying what's missing leaves reader unable to judge.
counter_arguments.source: my own reasoning, informed by WB-BNM report

missing_facts_top: Only 5.5% of flood victims had insurance; 22.5% no preparations (DOSM 2025).
PAC: PRAB Phase 1 RM129M for 5.6% forecasting accuracy. 3G shutdown affected 118 hydrological stations, only 43 replaced as of July 2024.
AG Report 2/2024: 145 of 712 water projects delayed 86-1,076 days at RM6.15B.
2021-2022 floods: RM6.1B (0.4% GDP), ~50 deaths, ~400,000 displaced — 10x the 2025 figure.
Kelantan 2024 residential damage RM139M; Pasir Mas RM46.2M, Tumpat RM31.7M, Kota Bharu RM19.2M.

gender_gaps.missing: Women bear caregiving burdens during/after floods; NADMA shows women+children >60% of evacuees; 2025 Kelantan deaths both women. Furniture counted (42.8% of household losses); care work not.

economic_gaps.missing: Aggregate household damage RM183.8M never disaggregated by income class. Flood-prone Klang Valley areas concentrated in <RM500,000 housing.
economic_gaps.why_matters: If flood damage is regressive, 'bill fell by half' obscures distributional impacts.

economic_gaps.missing: Business premises damage RM13.4M but SMEs lack business interruption insurance, may close permanently.
economic_gaps.why_matters: Permanent economic scarring in flood-prone communities.

geographic_gaps.missing: East Malaysia tops residential damage despite low population density. Sept 2025 landslide collapsed 275kV transmission tower in Sabah, blackout for ~1M people. Sarawak Belaga indigenous communities suing logging firms.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: East Malaysia flood dynamics differ — resource extraction governance, indigenous land rights, federal-state fiscal arrangements.

geographic_gaps.missing: Kelantan 2024 residential damage RM139M. Kelantan/Terengganu/Pahang consistently dominate every year — geographic persistence of vulnerability.
geographic_gaps.why_matters: Policy question shifts from 'did national bill fall?' to 'why do mitigation investments not reduce damage in most exposed states?'

overall_assessment: Stage 1 is disciplined in refusing causality and correctly identifies the mix-shift as the story. Scores 62/100 because it operates almost entirely within DOSM's frame — aggregate national figures with public/private split. Does not ask who within 'households' bears losses, whether 2025 drop reflects weather luck, why East Malaysia tops residential damage, what insurance data reveals, or how gender/class mediate impacts. Most consequential omission: multi-year DOSM data (2021-2025) showing annual damage dominated by whether a catastrophic event occurs.
